one_line_takeaway: Through a coded SoK of 59 papers, 21 production agent systems, and 26 plugins, the authors show a 0%-vs-67-76% adoption gap between academia-favored automated intent-alignment categories (intent anchoring, trust labeling) and industry-favored human-centric ones (policy specification, runtime approval, scope configuration), and argue this mismatch means agent security should be reframed as a human-computer-interaction design problem, not solved by removing humans from the loop.
key_finding: Adoption rates across the 21 production systems, from the paper's own Table 1: Scope & Boundary Configuration -- 4 papers, 16/21 systems (76%); Runtime Approval -- 5 papers, 15/21 systems (71%); Policy Specification -- 13 papers, 14/21 systems (67%); Trust & Data Labeling -- 8 papers, 0/21 systems (0%); Intent Anchoring -- 5 papers, 0/21 systems (0%). The paper frames this as a stark industry-academia mismatch: the three human-centric mechanisms industry deploys most are comparatively under-studied academically relative to their deployment share, while intent anchoring and trust labeling -- the categories academia favors as steps toward full automation -- have zero production adoption among the 21 systems surveyed. The paper also reports, from cited (not its own) work: AgentSpec's automated policy generation achieves only 70.96% recall (missing ~30% of necessary constraints); RTBAS prevents all targeted attacks with only 2% utility loss in controlled experiments yet has zero production adoption; AutoPermissions predicts user approval decisions with 85.1% accuracy from context; and interactive clarification improves task success by up to 74% over non-interactive settings (Vijayvargiya et al., cited). These are Related Work findings the authors cite as evidence for their position, not results the authors themselves produced.
core_contribution: A position/SoK paper (Wang, Li, Tian; UCLA) arguing that LLM agent security is fundamentally an agent-human interaction (AHI) problem rather than a purely algorithmic one. The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) a systematic comparison of two paths to intent alignment -- LLM-based automated verification (Path A) vs. human-in-the-loop expression (Path B) -- arguing human participation is currently indispensable; (2) a systematic mapping study (SoK) of 59 academic papers, 21 production agent systems, and 26 security plugins (as of April 2026) into a five-category AHI taxonomy (policy specification, runtime approval, scope/boundary configuration, trust/data labeling, intent anchoring), used to quantify a mismatch between what industry deploys and what academia studies; and (3) a three-direction research agenda (fatigue-resistant AHI mechanisms, usable policy/trust models, LLM-augmented human intent alignment) intended to make AHI security a first-class research subfield. The paper does not propose or evaluate a new defense system; its contribution is the taxonomy, the empirical corpus-level pattern, and the argumentative synthesis.
threat_model: The paper does not define a conventional adversarial threat model with attacker capabilities; as a position/SoK paper its 'threat' framing is the general agentic-security risk surface: prompt injection, LLM hallucination, misconfiguration, and value/goal drift causing an agent (e.g., Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Devin, Manus) to take actions misaligned with true user intent (unauthorized file access, data exfiltration, destructive commands). It treats intent alignment -- judging whether an agent action matches the user's actual goal -- as the unifying security problem underlying prompt injection defenses, policy engines, and access control in agent systems, and argues this problem cannot be solved by an LLM judge alone because of three cited failure modes: prompt imprecision, irreducible ambiguity, and circularity/attackability of LLM judges.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 6): (1) the SoK corpus is bounded by search scope (top-tier security/systems/ML/SE/NLP venues plus arXiv through April 2026) and may miss grey literature or other venues; (2) production-system analysis relies on public documentation, blogs, and system cards -- undisclosed internals may include additional undocumented mechanisms; (3) the five-category AHI taxonomy is not guaranteed to remain exhaustive as the field evolves; (4) the proposed research agenda is conceptual only -- no user studies or prototypes were built or evaluated by the authors. As a position paper, its central claims (e.g., that human participation is 'indispensable') rest on argumentation and corpus-level correlation (industry convergence on human-centric mechanisms) rather than controlled causal evidence; the paper itself acknowledges this is inference from a 'natural experiment,' not a designed one.

datasets_used: Author-constructed SoK corpus: 59 academic papers (2022-April 2026) from USENIX Security, S&P, CCS, NDSS, OSDI, SOSP, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, ICSE, FSE, ACL, EMNLP, and arXiv
21 production agent systems (e.g., Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Devin, Manus) coded from public documentation, engineering blogs, system cards, and incident reports
26 open-source/commercial security plugins/tools (MCP scanners, guardrail frameworks, governance toolkits)
quantitative_results: Scope & Boundary Configuration: 4 papers, 16/21 production systems (76%) -- paper's Table 1
Runtime Approval: 5 papers, 15/21 production systems (71%) -- paper's Table 1
Policy Specification: 13 papers, 14/21 production systems (67%) -- paper's Table 1
Trust & Data Labeling: 8 papers, 0/21 production systems (0%) -- paper's Table 1
Intent Anchoring: 5 papers, 0/21 production systems (0%) -- paper's Table 1
Policy Specification appears in 13 academic papers, 14 production systems, and 8 plugins (Appendix B.1 coding)
baselines_compared: No baselines are compared -- this is a taxonomy/position paper, not a defense system evaluated against baselines. Numeric results cited from third-party works (AgentSpec 70.96% recall, RTBAS 2% utility loss with all targeted attacks prevented, AutoPermissions 85.1% accuracy, interactive clarification +74% task success) are Related Work findings marshaled as supporting evidence, not baselines the authors themselves ran.
